overview.body: You've built City and Game classes. They work beautifully together. But Corpus Christi isn't just any city — it's the beach. It should have beach activities, a special arrival message, maybe even confetti. This week, you'll create WinCity, a specialized version of City that inherits everything City can do, then adds beach-specific features. Welcome to inheritance — the crown jewel of OOP.

mentorQuote.quote: You wrote the same game five different ways this semester. Each time it got better. That's what growth looks like. And now? WinCity is a City. One line of code, and you get all of City's powers for free. That's inheritance.

objectives: Polymorphism — same code works with City or WinCity
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 11: Inheritance
programRequirements: city.py MUST contain both City and WinCity classes
overview.filenameNote: Same four files as 5.2 — you'll modify city.py: city.py (City class + NEW WinCity subclass), game.py (no changes needed), function_library.py (no changes needed), and lab-5-3.py (updated game using WinCity).
overview.additionalFilesNote: CodeGrade will test that WinCity properly inherits from City and overrides methods correctly.

timeGuide.strategy: This is the shortest lab in Module 5 because you're building on top of working code. If your 5.1 and 5.2 are solid, inheritance is just adding a few lines. That's the power!

beforeYouBegin.intro: city.py will now contain TWO classes — City (unchanged) and WinCity (new, inherits from City). lab-5-3.py is a copy of lab-5-2.py with one key change: Corpus Christi becomes a WinCity instead of a regular City.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- city.py ----
class City:
    # Everything from Lab 5.1 unchanged
    __init__(self, name, city_type, tagline)
    get_name() / get_type() / get_tagline()
    add_connection() / get_distance_to() / get_destination_names()
    is_win_city() / is_dead_end()
    display_destinations() / __str__()

    # --- NEW method to add ---   [CP2]
    get_arrival_message(self)     returns 'Welcome to {name}!'


class WinCity(City):              inherits from City!   [CP1]

    __init__(self, name, tagline, beach_activities)
        City.__init__(self, name, 'WIN', tagline)   call parent!
        self.__beach_activities = beach_activities

    # --- New Methods ---   [CP2]
    get_beach_activities(self)        returns copy of activities list
    display_beach_activities(self)    prints numbered activity menu

    # --- Overridden Method ---   [CP2]
    get_arrival_message(self)     returns beach celebration message!


# ---- lab-5-3.py ----   [CP3]
from city import City, WinCity
from game import Game
from function_library import get_valid_int

create_cities()    Corpus Christi = WinCity(...) instead of City(...)
main()             win condition uses get_arrival_message() + display_beach_activities()

checkpoints.title: Create the WinCity Subclass
checkpoints.subtitle: Add a WinCity class to city.py that inherits from City.

content.body: A WinCity class that is a City but with an extra attribute for beach activities. One line — class WinCity(City): — gives you all of City's powers instantly.

Inheritance is how professional codebases stay maintainable. Instead of duplicating code, you build hierarchies where specialized classes extend base classes. Every game engine, web framework, and mobile app uses this pattern. Master it here and you're ready for software engineering.

content.role: Director of Technology, Bat City Collective
content.quote: Without inheritance, you'd have to copy the entire City class, paste it, rename it WinCity, then add your beach stuff. Every time you fix a bug in City, you'd fix it again in WinCity. Nightmare. Inheritance means WinCity automatically gets City's code. Fix City once, WinCity gets the fix for free.

references.description: You typed class Dog(Mammal): to make Dog inherit from Mammal, then called Mammal.__init__(self, species) inside Dog's __init__. WinCity follows the exact same pattern — inherit from City, call City's constructor.

items: Copy your city.py from Lab 5.2 (keep the entire City class exactly as-is)
At the BOTTOM of city.py (after the City class ends), add the WinCity class:

content.code:
class WinCity(City):
    """
    A winning destination city (beach!) that inherits from City.

    Adds beach-specific features while keeping all City functionality.
    """

    def __init__(self, name, tagline, beach_activities):
        """
        Initialize a WinCity.

        Args:
            name (str): City name
            tagline (str): City motto
            beach_activities (list): List of things to do at the beach
        """
        # Call the PARENT class constructor
        City.__init__(self, name, 'WIN', tagline)

        # Add WinCity-specific attribute
        self.__beach_activities = beach_activities

items: Notice: we pass 'WIN' as the city_type to City — WinCity is ALWAYS a WIN type, so the student doesn't have to specify it!

content.body: Step 1: Add this test code at the bottom of city.py:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
activities = ['Swimming', 'Building sandcastles', 'Eating seafood']
corpus = WinCity('Corpus Christi', 'Sparkling City by the Sea', activities)

print('Testing WinCity inheritance:')
print(f'  Name: {corpus.get_name()}')           # Inherited from City!
print(f'  Type: {corpus.get_type()}')           # Should be WIN
print(f'  Tagline: {corpus.get_tagline()}')     # Inherited from City!
print(f'  is_win_city: {corpus.is_win_city()}') # Inherited method!

content.output:
Testing WinCity inheritance:
  Name: Corpus Christi
  Type: WIN
  Tagline: Sparkling City by the Sea
  is_win_city: True

content.body: The magic: We never wrote get_name() or is_win_city() in WinCity — they're inherited from City! Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: No inheritance! WinCity won't have City's methods
Forgot self! Must pass self as first argument
Correct! Passes self to parent constructor

items: WinCity class defined after City class in city.py
class WinCity(City): syntax for inheritance
City.__init__(self, name, 'WIN', tagline) called in constructor
__beach_activities attribute initialized

content.body: A getter for beach activities, a display method, and an overridden get_arrival_message() that returns a special beach celebration instead of the generic City message.

references.description: Remember how Dog had make_sound() that returned 'Woof!' while Cat returned 'Meow!'? Same method name, different behavior. That's method overriding. WinCity will override get_arrival_message() to return a beach celebration!

items: First, add get_arrival_message() to the City class (base version — regular cities need arrival messages too!):

content.code:
# In the City class:
def get_arrival_message(self):
    """Return arrival message for this city."""
    return f'Welcome to {self.get_name()}!'

items: Add get_beach_activities() to WinCity:
Add display_beach_activities() to WinCity:
Override get_arrival_message() in WinCity — same method name, beach-specific behavior:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
activities = ['Swimming', 'Building sandcastles', 'Eating seafood']
corpus = WinCity('Corpus Christi', 'Sparkling City by the Sea', activities)
houston = City('Houston', 'HUB', 'Space City')

print('Testing method override (POLYMORPHISM!):')
print()
print('Regular City arrival message:')
print(f'  {houston.get_arrival_message()}')
print()
print('WinCity arrival message (overridden):')
print(f'  {corpus.get_arrival_message()}')
print()
corpus.display_beach_activities()

content.output:
Testing method override (POLYMORPHISM!):

Regular City arrival message:
  Welcome to Houston!

WinCity arrival message (overridden):
  YOU MADE IT TO CORPUS CHRISTI! THE BEACH AWAITS!

Things to do at the beach:
  - Swimming
  - Building sandcastles
  - Eating seafood

content.body: The magic: Same method name get_arrival_message(), different behavior based on object type. That's polymorphism! Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Return a copy with list(self.__beach_activities) to protect the original
Base City class needs it too — regular cities also need arrival messages

items: get_arrival_message() added to City class (base version)
get_beach_activities() in WinCity (returns copy)
display_beach_activities() in WinCity
get_arrival_message() overridden in WinCity with beach celebration

checkpoints.title: Update the Game
checkpoints.subtitle: Modify lab-5-3.py to use WinCity and display beach activities when you win.

content.body: An updated game that creates Corpus Christi as a WinCity object. When you reach it, the overridden arrival message displays automatically — that's polymorphism in action!

content.quote: This is the moment we've been building toward! When players finally reach Corpus Christi, they should feel like they've achieved something. The beach activities list makes the destination feel real and rewarding. Inheritance lets you create that special moment without changing how the rest of the game works. That's elegant design!

items: Copy your lab-5-2.py and rename it lab-5-3.py
Update the imports to include WinCity:

content.code:
from city import City, WinCity
from game import Game
from function_library import get_valid_int

items: In create_cities(), change Corpus Christi from a regular City to a WinCity:

content.code:
# OLD (regular City):
# corpus = City('Corpus Christi', 'WIN', 'Sparkling City by the Sea')

# NEW (WinCity with beach activities!):
beach_activities = [
    'Swimming in the Gulf',
    'Building sandcastles',
    'Eating fresh seafood',
    'Watching the sunset'
]
corpus = WinCity('Corpus Christi', 'Sparkling City by the Sea', beach_activities)

items: In your win condition, use the arrival message and display activities:

content.code:
if current_city.is_win_city():
    print()
    print(current_city.get_arrival_message())  # Polymorphism!
    print()
    current_city.display_beach_activities()     # WinCity-only method
    playing = False

finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 11 Sections 11.1–11.2. Inheritance, superclass constructors, method overriding, and polymorphism. All prior techniques from Modules 1–5 also permitted.

specific: Method overriding (same method name in subclass)
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Nothing — this is the MASTER week. You have access to the full course toolkit!

warnings: Submit ALL FOUR files: city.py (with WinCity), game.py, function_library.py, and lab-5-3.py. CodeGrade tests WinCity inheritance independently.
Make sure WinCity is defined AFTER City in city.py — Python reads top to bottom, City must exist before WinCity can inherit from it.

needHelp.chapterResource: Chapter 11 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every inheritance example; your primary reference for this lab
ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 11-9 (animals.py) — the Dog/Cat inheritance is exactly like WinCity/City
Don't forget self — City.__init__(self, ...) needs self as the first argument!
Put WinCity AFTER City — Python reads top to bottom; City must exist first
Test inheritance first — make sure inherited methods work before adding new ones
Submit to CodeGrade early — it tests inheritance specifically
